text:
# Industrial companies are popular prey for cybercriminals

[Analysis](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/analyses/index.html)

[Industry](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/industrie/index.html)

25/06/2025 • 4 minutes reading

[Martijn Mensonides](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/authors/martijn-mensonides/index.html)

[David Kemps](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/authors/david-kemps/index.html)

The manufacturing and food industries are vulnerable to cyber attacks due to extensive process automation and many digital links within the chain. Production lines that come to a standstill and stolen IP addresses can have enormous consequences. The pressure within the supply chain, growing digitalization, 'smart' machines, carrying out timely updates and sharing data with parties outside the factory are all risk factors that can attract digital criminals.

## Download the complete report

Read all the facts and developments in the report 'Industrial companies are popular prey for cyber criminals'.

- [Industrial companies are popular prey for cybercriminals

  249 KB](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/media/cyberveiligheid-industrie_tcm16-266609.pdf)

Although new technologies such as AI can strengthen security measures, they also bring new risks. It is therefore essential that companies in this sector improve their digital resilience to ensure that their processes can continue uninterrupted.

## Recent attacks in the sector

- In April 2023, [Nexperia](https://nos.nl/artikelen/2516457-chipmaker-nexperia-uit-nijmegen-gehackt), a chip manufacturer from Nijmegen, fell victim to ransomware. The hackers threatened to reveal valuable information, such as chip designs and customer data, if the company did not pay a ransom.
- In 2023, the Rotterdam chemical company [Chemours](https://www.handsonict.nl/kennis-media/blogs/phishing-attacks-in-de-industrie-recente-velden-en-lessen) was the target of a phishing attack, after which it had to deal with ransomware. Hackers sent emails that appeared to come from trusted internal sources within the organization. The shut down production lines led to significant financial losses.
- [VDL group](https://www.handsonict.nl/kennis-media/blogs/phishing-attacks-in-de-industrie-recente-velden-en-lessen) fell victim to a phishing attack in 2023. Fake internal company portals were used to obtain employee login details. This gave the hackers access to sensitive information, which they used to demand ransoms.
- On February 3, 2025, the Dutch production company [Tosaf](https://www.ccinfo.nl/menu-nieuws-trends/actuele-cyberattacks/2363994_slachtofferanalyse-en-trends-belgie-en-nederland-februari-2025) was attacked by ransomware. This resulted in production lines coming to a standstill, which put heavy pressure on operational management.
- In March 2024, hacker collective Stormous Group attacked the Belgian brewer [Duvel Moortgat](https://samendigitaalveilig.nl/nieuws/bierbrouwerij-duvel-slachtoffer-van-cyberattack-productie-valt-stil/#:~:text=Het%20hackerscollectief%20Stormous%20Group%20has,hackersgroep%20Stormous%20achter%20de%20attack.), known for beers such as Duvel, La Chouffe and De Koninck. They claimed to have stolen a lot of data and threatened to reveal it. The brewer did not provide details due to the investigation, but due to sufficient stock, deliveries were not jeopardized and production of all labels was quickly resumed.
- The Belgian coffee producer [Koffie Beyers](https://www.techzine.nl/nieuws/security/541947/grootste-belgische-koffiebranderij-slachtoffer-van-cyberattack/) was hit by ransomware in March 2024. The company produces approximately 35,000 tons of coffee beans annually and processes them into various products for major customers such as HEMA and LIDL. The extent of the damage is unknown.

## Cybersecurity in the sector

Research by ABN AMRO shows that 85 percent of industrial companies see cybercrime as a risk within their organization. One in ten respondents indicate that they have experienced operational disruptions due to cyber attacks. Phishing emails, invoice fraud and malware are particularly common forms of cybercrime.

The same research shows that industrial companies are actively investing in cybersecurity. Of respondents, 48 ​​percent indicate they will spend more in 2024, with half indicating that their investments have remained approximately the same compared to 2023. The main reason for the increase in spending is the growing threat of cyber attacks and the need to comply with legal requirements such as the NIS2 directive and the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR).

The challenges within the sector mainly lie in the rapid emergence of new threats, employee discipline and costs. A majority of companies (61 percent) see AI as an opportunity to strengthen cybersecurity. For example, AI can analyze massive amounts of data to identify suspicious activity so organizations can prevent attacks. At the same time, 57 percent see AI as a possible threat to their organization. For example, hackers use AI to refine their attacks. This makes the attacks faster, more efficient and harder to detect.

## NIS2: new European legislation must improve cyber resilience

The NIS2, the successor to the Network and Information Security Directive (NIS), will come into effect in the European Union from October 2024. This European Union (EU) directive aims to improve the cybersecurity of key services in member states. The Netherlands has not succeeded in transposing the EU directives into national laws within the specified period. However, national legislation, consisting of the Cybersecurity Act (Cbw/NIS2) and the Resilience of Critical Entities Act (Wwke/CER), is underway. These laws are expected to come into effect in the third quarter of 2025.

Research by ABN AMRO shows that 48 percent of industrial companies are not familiar with the NIS2 obligations. At the same time, 35 percent indicate that they can fully comply with the obligations of NIS2. If the NIS2 obligations are not met, there is a risk of [heavy fines](https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/NL/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32022L2555&from=EN#d1e3946-80-1). These fines can amount to 10 million euros or 2 percent of global annual turnover.

[Read also - Cybersecurity Act: prepare yourself for stricter rules](/nl/zakelijk/insights/cybersecurity/veiligheid/cybersecuritywet-prepare-je-voor-op-stricter-rules.html)

## More information

Curious whether your organization falls under the NIS2 guideline? Read our report: ['Cyber ​​resilience of entrepreneurs lags behind reality'](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/alle-sectoren/cyberweerbaarheid-ondernemers-blijft-achter-bij-reueel.html), or complete the [NIS2 Self-Evaluation](https://regelhulpenvoorbedrijven.nl/NIS-2-NL/) of the National Cyber ​​Security Center.

## Read more in the industrial sector

The industry is a heavyweight in the Dutch economy. Export is an important driver for this, but at the same time creates uncertainty. There are also themes that have a positive influence on growth, such as cooperation in the chain, innovation and sustainability.
